data.title: How Hong Kong’s last remaining porcelain factory is keeping city’s guangcai heritage alive
data.description:
Brush in hand, eyes focused, Hongkonger Anita Tso is in the zone as she carefully paints a fish on a delicate porcelain bowl. It is a design she has drawn countless times.
“I have been painting porcelain for more than 40 years – it is my life,” she says.
The craft is in her blood. Tso’s family runs Yuet Tung China Works in Hong Kong’s Kowloon Bay district. It was the first porcelain factory in Hong Kong, and it is the only one that remains in the city.
Established in 1928 by her grandfather Tsao Lui-chung, Yuet Tung China Works – previously known as Kam Wah Loong – specialises in guangcai, a Qing-dynasty-era technique originating from Guangzhou, a sprawling port city in southern China’s Guangdong province.
Located on the third floor of the Kowloon Industrial Centre in Kowloon Bay, the factory’s sprawling showroom – which encompasses four shops – features floor-to-ceiling shelves stacked with plates, bowls, tea sets and vases, many of which are adorned with traditional motifs such as flowers, dragons, phoenixes and carp.

Weaving through the narrow passageways is akin to exploring a giant treasure trove. But it is a tight squeeze, so tread carefully – Yuet Tung China Works has a “you break, you buy” policy.
Artisans paint away in quiet alcoves, applying brushstrokes acquired from years of practice. In a way, Yuet Tung China Works is a living museum of a fading art.
And there is more than just what is on show in Kowloon Bay.
“We have warehouses around the city that contain millions of pieces,” Tso says.
Passed down through four generations, Yuet Tung China Works has played a significant role in the development and preservation of traditional Chinese porcelain painting for almost 100 years.
Yuet Tung’s prized porcelain has graced the tables of royal families in France and Britain. In Hong Kong, it is the porcelain of choice for luxury hotels such as the Four Seasons, Mandarin Oriental, Shangri-La and The Peninsula.

Pieces have also found their way to the residences of former Hong Kong governors, such as Chris Patten and Murray MacLehose. In fact, MacLehose’s wife was so taken with the company’s craftsmanship that she commissioned a set of family tableware in 1975. Its pattern, a blue flower that was based on a design on a plate owned by the MacLehose family, became known as the “Governor Crest” and remains popular to this day.
Another popular design, Tso says, is “Canton Rose”, a classic combination of petals, buds and green leaves.
“Thousand Flowers” is another traditional pattern, but the company stopped hand-painting the design decades ago due to labour shortages and high production costs. In 2020, the design was reintroduced thanks to the use of a transfer printing technique for mass production. The final touch, a glossy gold finish, is added by hand.
Porcelain is integral to Hong Kong’s cultural history, the trade booming after the 1960s when craftsmen fled to the city in response to political unrest in mainland China.
Painted porcelain factories could be found across Kowloon and Hong Kong Island, including three Yuet Tung China Works.
The company reached its peak in the 1960s and ’70s, when about 100 painters were employed, with most demand for the painted porcelain coming from Europe, the US and the Middle East.

Business declined in the 1980s amid growing competition from mainland China. A drop in the number of skilled artisans and a lack of successors, coupled with fewer young people willing to dedicate themselves to mastering the complex craft, added to the challenges.
The hand-painting process was also costly and time-consuming.
While the art faces an uphill battle, with its fate closely tied to the survival of places like Yuet Tung, dedicated individuals and community efforts are working to preserve its legacy for future generations, even though its large-scale commercial presence is largely gone.
It is much needed. Part of Hong Kong’s intangible cultural heritage, guangcai is only hanging on by a thread thanks to exhibitions, workshops and collaborations with brands.
Earlier this year, the M+ museum in the West Kowloon Cultural District hosted a talk on guangcai, while Crafts on Peel, a charitable organisation and creative hub in Central dedicated to preserving, reviving and reimagining traditional craftsmanship, also hosts workshops and exhibitions focusing on guangcai.

The Leisure and Cultural Services Department’s recent “Muse Fest HK 2025” also celebrated the craft with workshops.
Guangcai emerged in the late Kangxi period of the Qing dynasty and has since developed over a history spanning more than 300 years.
The art of guangcai involves the painting of intricate patterns and the use of rich colours, especially a wide range of pinks, greens, yellows and reds, on shaped and fired white porcelain “blanks” sourced from China’s porcelain capital, Jingdezhen, a city in eastern Jiangxi province. The pieces are then fired at a temperature of 800 degrees Celsius (1,472 degrees Fahrenheit) to seal the overglaze decorations.
Master painters first outline the designs in black and then carefully fill in the colours. The technique is also known for its liberal use of gilt or gold trim.

Guangcai was traditionally made for export to Western markets, hence its name as “export porcelain”. It blends Chinese and Western cultural elements and painting styles.
It is common for artisans to incorporate Western-requested motifs, such as family crests or specific landscapes, alongside traditional Chinese auspicious imagery, including butterflies, birds and flowers.
The composition is a difficult skill to master. In the past, masters could accurately outline intricate patterns with bare hands without requiring any modification.
Yuet Tung China Works, Unit 1-3, 3/F, Kowloon Bay Industrial Centre, 15 Wang Hoi Road, Kowloon Bay. Tel: 2796 1125

data.title: Why Asean’s AI ambition may lead to widening economic and tech divide
data.description:
As countries and economies around the world scramble for a slice of the growing artificial intelligence pie, Southeast Asia is also aiming to usher in a new dawn of higher productivity, fuelled by more investment and research opportunities in the technology.
But tech experts warn that a deepening economic and tech divide is looming as nations in the region adopt AI at a different pace.
On Wednesday, Google DeepMind announced the opening of a research lab in Singapore, focused on researching and developing existing AI models such as Gemini.
“Southeast Asian markets make up some of the highest AI adopters in the world,” Lila Ibrahim, chief operating officer of DeepMind, said. “The fact that the hub here is now in Singapore makes us closer to the ground to be able to respond to that, to develop partnerships and so on.”
It is DeepMind’s first hub in Southeast Asia and third in Asia-Pacific, with its labs in Japan and India having started operations earlier this year.
It joins other tech titans that have invested in AI research and development efforts in the region. In July in Singapore, Microsoft opened a lab and Alibaba Cloud launched its innovation hub. Last year, Malaysia pledged US$2.2 billion to develop its AI infrastructure.
AI could potentially raise the region’s gross domestic product by 10 to 18 per cent by 2030, through benefits such as productivity gains and revenue increments, according to a 2020 study by consulting firm Kearney.

Uneven gains
Although projections on AI’s potential by various research houses have painted a rosy picture, the technological race could leave developing countries in Asean behind, analysts warn.
Some economies in the Association of Southeast Asian Nations that have more advanced digital infrastructure and talent, such as Singapore and Malaysia, are better positioned to harness gains, they note. In contrast, less technologically savvy countries in the region face challenges such as weaker talent pipelines and a lack of regulatory frameworks.
Lili Yan Ing, secretary general of the International Economic Association, said Asean was already experiencing a rapidly widening digital divide, noting that Singapore was far ahead in private funding and start-up numbers. Countries such as Cambodia, Laos and Myanmar remained far behind in digital infrastructure and human capital, she added.
According to a report by Google, Temasek and Bain & Company released earlier this month, 495 AI start-ups have laid roots in Singapore since 2020, compared with 10 and 20 in the Philippines and Thailand, respectively.
Singapore also attracted more than 55 per cent of private funding in AI start-ups in the region, amounting to US$1.31 billion in the 12 months ending June.
As adoption levels were already uneven and structural gaps were deep, Asean faced a greater risk of rising inequality, Ing said, adding that the divide was likely to widen in the near term.
AI would affect manual workers, particularly in countries where such jobs are dominant across sectors, according to observers.
Workers in occupations involving rigid routines, as well as screen-based and codifiable tasks, were most vulnerable to being replaced by AI, and these jobs were prevalent in Asean economies such as Indonesia, the Philippines and Vietnam, said Calvin Cheng, director of economics, trade and regional integration at the Institute of Strategic and International Studies in Malaysia.

These economies are also less likely to reap AI’s productivity dividends because they have a lower proportion of jobs that could be made more productive by AI, according to Cheng.
The Philippines’ business process outsourcing sector faced the highest risk, said Ing from the International Economic Association, citing studies stating that up to 40 per cent of the sector’s tasks could be automated with generative AI. The sector employs nearly 2 million people and accounts for about 7 per cent of the country’s GDP.
AI had also started to displace jobs in the retail and transport sectors through e-commerce automation and route optimisation, especially in Indonesia and Thailand, she added.
Meanwhile, Malaysia has emerged as the front runner in Southeast Asia’s data centre race. The country accounts for over half of planned capacity in the region, and its government aims to house 81 data centres by 2035.
Developing countries might not have enough resources to build such centres to develop complex AI systems, said Miguel Alberto Gomez, a senior research fellow at the Centre on Asia and Globalisation in the Lee Kuan Yew School of Public Policy.
“To have adequate computational power, we need to purchase semiconductor assets and microchips,” he said.
These microchips, known as graphics processing units (GPU), provide computing power for creating AI models, and their cost has soared due to surging demand.
Referring to the Philippines’ recent slate of power outages, Gomez said Asean members faced a major challenge of meeting the needs of energy-guzzling data centres.

Asean-wide AI adoption
To make growth more inclusive, Asean should cooperate through governance, education and sharing of resources, according to analysts.
They cite the development of large language models in Singapore, such as Sea-Lion, which recognises 13 languages, including Javanese, Thai and Vietnamese, and has reportedly been downloaded more than 230,000 times in the region.
DeepMind’s new lab plans to focus on research to improve its models’ capabilities in understanding regional languages and cultural nuances.
Uneven levels of AI adoption could also undermine political and social stability in the region, as developing countries often lack a framework to deal with threats posed by the technology, analysts say.
Generative AI risked being used to generate misinformation and malware to conduct cyberattacks, as well as identify recruits susceptible to radicalism, said Asha Hemrajani, a senior fellow at the S. Rajaratnam School of International Studies.
“It’s easy for AI to make a deepfake of an election candidate saying something inappropriate and distribute it, and that might affect the way voters think and ultimately impact democracy.”
Some Asean governments might find themselves increasingly dependent on foreign tech giants, risking the erosion of their sovereignty, said Simon Chesterman, senior director of AI governance at AI Singapore.

Chesterman advised nations against pursuing AI sovereignty by acquiring huge amounts of GPUs or training large models, calling such an approach “economically unrealistic and strategically unwise”.
“Those funds are often better spent on diversifying access to models, improving data quality and governance, and training local talent who can adapt AI to national priorities.”
Gomez said focusing on building on individual parts of the AI chain, such as building and training human capital, might also make the region more attractive to foreign investors.
“No single state would be able to dominate AI in the region,” Gomez said. “The advantage of Asean is that all of its states are interested in AI” so the bloc could adopt a collaborative approach, he added.
Hemrajani said the adoption of AI was not driven solely by money, but also by the political will of governments to come up with strategies based on their strengths.
“A country may not have the resources to build mega data centres, but it can play a different role in the AI food chain, like developing and equipping its young people with AI-related skills,” she said.
Gomez said states should carefully map out their plans to use the fast-developing technology instead of setting “large aspirational goals like societal transformation”.
“I think such perspectives are problematic, especially when you consider the economic costs of getting to that point and how much Southeast Asian states would have to balance existing priorities with AI development,” he said.
“Figure out what you want and how to get there is a necessary first step, and collaborate with the region to fill in gaps and continue from there.”

data.title: Hong Kong table tennis player Doo to be flag bearer at National Games closing ceremony
data.description:
Hong Kong table tennis player Doo Hoi-kem will be the city’s flag bearer at the National Games closing ceremony on Friday, which is being held at the Huanle Theatre in Shenzhen.
The 15th edition of the multi-sport event is coming to a close after almost two weeks of action in various venues and stadiums across Guangzhou, Hong Kong and Macau.
With the ceremony less than three hours away, a government source confirmed to the Post that the honour of carrying the Bauhinia flag had fallen to the Olympic bronze medallist
Doo, who will turn 29 next Thursday, took part in two events at the National Games. She was eliminated – alongside Wong Chun-ting – in the mixed doubles quarter-final by Hebei pair Zhou Yu and Zhan Xiaotong. And was knocked out of the second round of the women’s singles by Liu Fei of Liaoning.
A four-time world championships mixed doubles bronze medallist, Doo was among the women’s team that claimed bronze at the Tokyo Olympic Games in 2021.

data.title: 11 injured in Canada as grizzly bear attacks students, teachers on walking trail
data.description:
A grizzly bear attacked a group of schoolchildren and teachers on a walking trail in British Columbia, Canada, injuring 11 people, two of them critically.
The attack happened on Thursday afternoon in Bella Coola, 700km (435 miles) northwest of Vancouver. The Nuxalk Nation said the “aggressive bear” remained on the loose on Thursday evening and police and conservation officers were on the scene.
“Officers are armed. Remain indoors and off the highway,” the First Nation said in a social media post.
Two people were critically injured and two had serious injuries, Emergency Health Services spokesman Brian Twaites said. The others were treated at the scene.
Parent Veronica Schooner said a lot of people tried to halt the attack but one male teacher “got the whole brunt of it” and was among the people taken by helicopter from the scene.

Schooner’s 10-year-old son Alvarez was in the class of fourth- and fifth-graders that was attacked and was so close to the animal “he even felt its fur,” she said.
“He said that bear ran so close to him, but it was going after somebody else,” Schooner said.
She added that some children were hit with bear spray as the teachers fought off the bear and Alvarez was limping and his shoes were muddy from running for safety. Her son’s thoughts, however, were with his classmates.
“He keeps crying for his friends, and oh my goodness, right away he started praying for his friends,” she added.
Acwsalcta School, an independent school run by Nuxalk First Nation in Bella Coola, said in a social media post that the school would be closed on Friday and counselling made available.
“It’s hard to know what to say during this very difficult time. We are so grateful for our team and our students,” the post said.

data.title: US$44 million sale of Hong Kong luxury home linked to mainland Chinese investor
data.description:
A buyer bearing the same name as veteran investor Xu Liusheng is the latest owner of a luxury home in Hong Kong after acquiring a HK$342 million (US$43.9 million) unit in the affluent Southern district, with the transaction adding to a growing list of prime assets being snapped up following years of falling prices.
The buyer shares the name of the owner of Shenzhen Kaifu Investment, who is known for acquiring shares in public companies in mainland China. Shenzhen Kaifu was recently deregistered, according to Qichacha, a business information platform in China.
The Deep Water Bay property – with 3,927 sq ft of saleable area – was purchased on November 14 and handed over to Xu on Thursday, according to Land Registry records. The deal translated to HK$87,089 per square foot.
Xu paid HK$10 million less than the HK$352 million the property’s previous owner, Chen Sze-lok, paid in 2015.

The unit, House 2, features gardens, carports and terraces. It was part of an exclusive development that was completed in 1986 and comprised 12 three-storey homes, according to information on the website of luxury property agency List Sotheby’s International Realty.
The sale marked the second transaction in the luxury project this year. The 3,889 sq ft House 3 changed hands for HK$319 million – or HK$82,026 per square foot – in September.
Shenzhen Kaifu’s Xu, born in 1968, rose to prominence in the late 1990s as a major investor in mainland public companies. In 2017, he was fined more than 110 million yuan (US$15.47 million) by the China Securities Regulatory Commission for allegedly manipulating over 30 stocks.
Since then, Xu has rarely appeared in the news. He still held shares in about 40 mainland companies, according to data on online platform East Money.
Hong Kong’s recovering high-end property sector has been attracting end-users and investors. Earlier this week, Matrix Properties, a company associated with Vincent Gao Leyi, founder of Hong Kong-based hedge fund CoreView Capital, took possession of a 9,414 sq ft six-bedroom unit at the upscale development No 15 Shouson Hill, also in the Southern district, Land Registry documents showed.
According to the Companies Registry, Gao served as a director of Matrix Properties, along with Jiang Yijun. Gao was also the CEO and chief investment officer of CoreView, according to his LinkedIn page. Jiang and Bosco Consultancy, with a registered address in the British Virgin Islands, were listed as shareholders of Matrix Properties.
Hong Kong’s super luxury property market recorded 35 transactions worth about HK$23 billion in the 19 months to August, according to Savills. Ownership has shifted from local property and industry tycoons to mainland China buyers who built their fortunes in fast-growing industries.
Mainland China buyers accounted for 80 per cent of the deals worth HK$300 million or more – a total of 24 in 2024 and nine in the first seven months of this year – the consultancy said.
The buying spree coincided with falling prices in the high-end segment. Properties with areas of at least 1,076 sq ft fell by as much as 22.7 per cent from their peak in October 2021, according to government data. Prices have inched up by 0.03 per cent so far this year.
Hong Kong ranked fourth globally for sales of homes worth US$10 million in the third quarter, with 56 transactions, according to Knight Frank. The city trailed Dubai’s 103, New York’s 74 and Los Angeles’ 64. Hong Kong’s luxury home deals amounted to US$1.04 billion, the consultancy said in a report on Monday.

data.title: Multilateralism isn’t dead, despite a Trumpian year
data.description:
As South Africa’s President Cyril Ramaphosa convenes the Group of 20 summit in Johannesburg, two things are likely to be on many minds: the empty chair and the spirit of ubuntu.
Wikipedia defines the ubuntu philosophy as encompassing “the interdependence of humans on one another and the acknowledgement of one’s responsibility to their fellow humans and the world around them”. For the purposes of South Africa’s hosting of the G20 summit, that Zulu term means multilateralism – anathema to US President Donald Trump’s “America first” philosophy.
The chair next to the G20 host normally seats the incoming chairman, in this case, Trump – but he has declared a US boycott. “I don’t want to hand over to an empty chair, but the empty chair will be there,” said Ramaphosa in Soweto last week.
Trump’s stated reason is the unsubstantiated claim of “white genocide” in South Africa: “Afrikaners…are being killed and slaughtered, and their land and farms are being illegally confiscated,” he wrote on social media: “No US government official will attend as long as these human rights abuses continue.”
The true reason is that Trump’s passionate preference for deal-making unilateralism is deeply at odds with the G20’s preference for cooperation and multilateralism. Or in Ramaphosa’s terms, ubuntu.
It also does not help that South Africa’s G20 chairmanship – following in the footsteps of previous hosts Indonesia, India and Brazil – focuses on addressing climate change, reducing poor countries’ debt burdens, financing energy transitions and ensuring that the rush for critical minerals benefits producers. The United States has reportedly indicated it would veto any G20 mention of climate change.

The G20 summit, coming a year after Trump’s re-election, reflects the radical changes his administration aims to impose on international trade and the world economy. It provides an appropriate moment to audit the impact of Trump’s second term, and where it is set to take us.
First, Trump has been true to his word on using tariffs as a wedge by which to dismantle rules-based multilateralism and replace it with his scatological version of deal-based unilateralism. The World Trade Organization, marginalised and made moribund by Trump’s unilateralist agenda, reported last week that the amount of G20 goods imports covered by trade “measures” – mostly tariffs – has risen fourfold since mid-October last year to around US$2.6 trillion.
Average tariffs have soared, but exact numbers are hard to pin down because of rates differing sharply by country, delayed implementation, broad product exclusions and legal uncertainties around enforcement. Rather than burst the trade balloon, Trump has squeezed it in the middle, prompting trading partners to redirect rather than reduce exports.
The US trade deficit that so irritates Trump seems as large as ever, with American consumers mainly paying the price of higher tariffs. But vacillation and procrastination means it is yet unclear where his tariff strategy will ultimately take us. Evidence so far suggests Trump, and the global economy, may still pay a high price.


WTO director general Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala has provided a smidgen of good news, however: “Even as the global trading system endures the most severe disruptions in 80 years, trade is showing considerable resilience as most WTO members continue to trade normally with each other.”
Despite the Trump disruption, the WTO expects the global goods trade to rise by 2.4 per cent this year. And given his eccentric obsession with trade in goods rather than services, the global commercial services trade is expected to continue to grow, by 4.6 per cent this year.
Trump’s science-starved climate change denial, coupled by his determination to boost US oil and gas production, has seriously set back global efforts to reduce carbon emissions and restrain global warming. Already, increasingly violent weather, triggering unprecedented storms, floods, droughts and wildfires, has forced others to shift focus towards managing impact rather than cutting or controlling emissions.
The Trump 2.0 concern over national security has not only provided a pretext for tariff protections but, coupled with the terrible conflicts in Ukraine and Gaza, has also significantly lifted defence spending. This is alarming in itself but, in boosting debt in many economies, also inevitably diverts funds urgently needed for climate change, the containment of pandemic threats and the general priorities of education, social services and infrastructure building.


So far, Trump’s erratic leadership style has cost the US’ international reputation dear. Pollster Ipsos, in a survey across 29 countries, found that America’s reputation as a force for good slumped from 59 per cent in September and October last year to 46 per cent in April this year, with the largest falls recorded among traditional allies like Canada and across Europe. For the first time, China’s reputation has risen above that of the US, to 49 per cent.
In Singapore, a team from the Lee Kuan Yew School of Public Policy reached similar conclusions about the 10 members of the Association of Southeast Asian Nations in an Asean study spanning three decades: “Southeast Asia is starting to choose – and the direction of travel is in favour of China.”
So what can we conclude after one year of Trump 2.0? Much harm, some unlikely to be reversible. But a majority of the harm is being inflicted inside the US. Despite the duress, most countries clearly prefer Ramaphosa’s ubuntu to Trump’s unilateralism. Multilateralism is not dead but, as the G20 meeting will show, it walks with a heavy limp.

data.title: What is oshikatsu? Nearly 50% of Japan’s young workers seek trend for fulfilment
data.description:
Nearly half of Japanese full-time workers in their 20s engage in oshikatsu – a practice that involves devoting time to pop or sports idols, anime characters or hobbies such as collecting model trains – according to a recent recruitment survey.
The findings, released on Friday by Mynavi Corp, indicate that the trend – spanning everything from social media posts to buying merchandise and attending fan events or concerts – has shifted from a niche subculture into the mainstream and could even bolster workplace motivation.
The survey, conducted in October, gathered around 20,000 responses from employees in their 20s to 50s nationwide. While the overall oshikatsu participation rate was 28.5 per cent, it reached 49.2 per cent among workers in their 20s and declined steadily across older age groups.
Pollsters attribute the trend’s rise to the spread of social media and a wider desire among young Japanese workers for more balance in their lives. The term oshikatsu, which translates to “activities that support my favourite”, gained traction online during the pandemic before entering mainstream Japanese usage.


Nearly 80 per cent of participants in the recent survey said oshikatsu brought them fulfilment, and more than 60 per cent said it helped motivate them at work.
The average expenditure by fans on such activities was more than 13,000 yen (US$84) monthly.
In April, Reuters reported that oshikatsu had become a significant driver of consumer spending in Japan, with some 14 million participants contributing an estimated 3.5 trillion yen to the economy each year.
The wave has caught the attention of major corporations eager to cash in on this consumer segment.
Fabio Gygi, a senior lecturer in anthropology at the University of London, noted in a commentary for The Conversation, an academic opinion and analysis platform, that pandemic-era supply chain disruption and geopolitical shocks had “caused Japanese consumers to reduce their spending”.
“However, with wages set to rise again for the third time in three years, the government is cautiously optimistic that economic growth can be rekindled through consumer-driven spending. Entertainment and media companies are looking to oshikatsu as a potential driver of this,” Gygi added.

Inflation proof
In a Japan Times report in September, Kohei Okazaki, chief market economist at Nomura Securities, said the trend was proving resilient even as prices rose, noting that “oshikatsu-related consumption is expanding due to consumers’ desire to give support, regardless of cost.”
The report highlighted apparel retailer Shimamura as one of the companies attracting investor interest for its focus on products featuring popular characters and collaborations with influencers.
Shimamura’s share price this year reached its highest level in more than twenty-five years.
Investors have also flocked to firms with strong character-related intellectual property, including Sanrio – owner of Hello Kitty, My Melody and Kuromi – while Bandai Namco Holdings reported higher revenue and profit from April to June on strong sales tied to the mecha anime series Mobile Suit Gundam.

data.title: 34 arrested in Hong Kong crackdown on illegal workers, including 13 ex-helpers
data.description:
Hong Kong immigration officers have arrested 34 people, including 13 former domestic helpers who overstayed their visas, during an operation to crack down on illegal labour.
The operation is the third in two months in which current and former domestic helpers were caught working illegally.
The Immigration Department said on Friday that officers conducted a three-day anti-illegal work operation from Tuesday to Thursday, carrying out raids on multiple locations across the city, including restaurants and food manufacturing plants.
Officers arrested 34 people, including 24 illegal workers, two overstayers and eight others suspected of employing illegal workers.
The arrested illegal workers included one man and 23 women aged 22 to 52. Among them were 13 former domestic helpers who overstayed their visas, 10 holders of recognisance forms, which prohibited them from taking any employment in the city, and one mainland Chinese visitor who overstayed a visa.
Most of those arrested were engaged in jobs such as dishwashing, cleaning and kitchen work, with the longest stint lasting nearly a year. They earned daily wages of HK$300 (US$38.50) to HK$500.
Two other overstayers arrested were Indonesian women aged 23 and 38.

The remaining eight arrested for allegedly employing illegal workers included seven men and one woman, aged 27 to 64, and they were owners or people in charge of the companies or restaurants involved.
“The Immigration Department will continue to actively follow up on the investigation, and we do not rule out that more people will be arrested or prosecuted in connection with the case,” said Mo Chuen-kit, the department’s deputy commander of the task force.
He reminded employers that all domestic helpers who had been dismissed and whose contracts had been terminated early or had expired were prohibited from working in Hong Kong.
The government amended the Immigration Ordinance in 2021 to increase penalties for employers who hired illegal workers, raising the maximum penalty from a fine of HK$350,000 and imprisonment for three years to a fine of HK$500,000 and 10 years’ imprisonment.

data.title: In Malaysia’s Kedah, Chinese text at factory event sparks double-standard accusation
data.description:
A senior official in Malaysia’s northern state of Kedah has defended the use of Chinese characters on a foundation stone for a tyre plant, after the inscription drew criticism in a country where public displays of Chinese text can become flashpoints over national identity and political loyalty.
The controversy began after photos circulated of a foundation stone bearing Chinese characters at the groundbreaking ceremony for Prinx Tire Malaysia, the local subsidiary of Chinese tyre maker Prinx Chengshan, at Kedah Rubber City (KRC) earlier this week. The 2.6 billion ringgit (US$627 million) plant is set to become the first anchor investment at the 1,244-acre industrial estate.
The photos have prompted a wave of online criticism, with some social media users accusing Kedah’s ruling Pan-Malaysian Islamic Party (PAS) of applying a double standard. Critics noted that leaders from the Islamist party have previously pushed for stricter use of Malay and Jawi – the Arabic-derived script used for writing the Malay language – on commercial signboards.
One widely shared comment on social media read: “Even the foundation stone is in Chinese. PAS claims to uphold Jawi – what a bunch of liars, phui. If this happened in Penang or Selangor, their supporters would be screaming nonstop.”
Defending the move, Haim Hilman Abdullah, the state executive councillor in charge of industry and investment and a PAS central committee member, said in a social media post that the foundation stone also carried Malay text and all official speeches at the ceremony were delivered in Malay.
He urged the public “not to be worried” about the Chinese inscription, which he said simply read “laying the foundation”, and argued that Malaysia should show mutual respect when hosting foreign investors.
“These foreign investors [from China] bring in new technology, job opportunities and revenue for the people. As hosts, we respect their identity, the same way they respect our diverse languages and cultures,” he wrote in his social media post, as quoted by Free Malaysia Today.

He added: “PAS and PN are not static. We have never hindered progress, never restricted anyone’s right to be religious or ethnic. We defend the national identity, but at the same time open the door to billions of ringgit in investments for the future of the people.”
Haim also used the post to appeal to voters in Sabah, saying that if they wanted a “more stable, more developed and more open future”, they should support PAS and the broader Perikatan Nasional coalition.
Prinx’s Kedah facility marks a significant step for KRC, which the federal government launched in 2022 to develop a dedicated rubber-industry hub in Malaysia’s northern region. At its inauguration, then prime minister Ismail Sabri Yaakob said the park was expected to generate around 15,000 jobs and attract 10 billion ringgit in investment.
The language debate echoes a wider national conversation over multilingual signage in Malaysia. Last year, former prime minister Mahathir Mohamad complained on social media that shopping centres in Kuala Lumpur featured “big” Chinese characters with no Malay wording, saying that “suddenly I felt I was in China”.
He questioned whether the country had “become a part of China”, sparking heated online exchanges in which many Malaysians accused him of politicising the issue and noted that the signs were aimed at tourists rather than signalling any erosion of national identity.

data.title: What to see at Hong Kong Palace Museum’s ancient Egypt exhibition – 5 of the best exhibits
data.description:
From a sculpture of a nine-year-old pharaoh to cat mummies and an ancient tablet comparable to the Rosetta Stone, a new Hong Kong Palace Museum exhibition sheds light on ancient Egyptian civilisation.
Opening November 20 and running until August 31, 2026, “Ancient Egypt Unveiled: Treasures from Egyptian Museums” showcases 250 artefacts, many loaned from the Egyptian Museum in Cairo, with some having never been removed from Egyptian soil before this tour.
Beyond the national treasures, the exhibition – which marks the 70th anniversary of China-Egypt diplomatic relations next year – will also feature more than 10 multimedia installations, a series of public talks, workshops and guided tours.
“This exhibition is more than a display. It is a dialogue between past and present, between Egypt and the world, and between two great cultures united by reverence for timeless beauty and human creativity,” says Dr Mohamed Ismail Khaled, secretary general of the Supreme Council of Antiquities (SCA) in Egypt.

Here are five unmissable highlights:
1. Colossal statue of Tutankhamun in quartzite
The star of the show is the colossal statue of Tutankhamun in quartzite.
The boy king, who ascended to the throne at age nine and died nine years later, was most celebrated for restoring Egypt’s traditional polytheistic beliefs. His modern fame skyrocketed with the discovery of his tomb in 1922, which remained nearly entirely intact for more than three millennia.
This sculpture was discovered at the Egyptian site of Medinet Habu, within the remnants of a temple to his successors, Ay and Horemheb.

Standing three metres (9.8 feet) tall, the statue has youthful features that are atypical for its symmetrical pharaonic counterparts. It depicts the young pharaoh wearing a traditional nemes headdress, adorned with royal blue and gold stripes and a broad usekh collar. A falcon-headed dagger is tucked into the waistband of his royal kilt, also known as a shendyt.
Notably, his throne name, inscribed on the belt clip, appears to have been usurped with the cartouche or symbol of Horemheb. The false beard is mostly missing, though its outline remains clear.
The display is particularly eye-catching for its implementation of a special projection-mapping technology, which digitally restores the damaged portions. The light projection reveals what appears to be an intact sculpture, with a reconstructed face and limbs, and vibrant blue and gold paint.
The statue can be found in the second section dedicated to the pharaoh, titled “The World of Tutankhamun”.
2. Mummies of animals, specifically cats and cattle

The mummification of animals was a widespread practice in ancient Egypt, embedded in a religious world view that blurred the boundaries between the human, the divine and the natural world.
During the Ptolemaic period (332BC-30BC), worship for the feline goddess Bastet – often depicted as a fierce lioness deity of the home, fertility and benevolence – reached its peak. Ancient Egyptians offered mummified cats as gifts to seek the favour of the goddess.
The cat mummies on display vary in presentation. X-rays reveal that while some mummies contain complete cat skeletons, others hold only fragmentary bones or are stuffed with other materials, such as reeds and linen.
Differences were also found in burial methods. Some cats were placed in ornate coffins or bronze statues, while others were wrapped in linen and buried in collective, stacked layers.
Alongside them is a carefully crafted calf mummy and cartonnage, or mummy case, constructed over a wooden frame, which also served a religious purpose as it is associated with the goddess Isis, commonly known as the mother of the divine Apis bull. Calves were seen as a living incarnation of the deity and lived lavishly inside temples.

X-ray analysis confirms it contains the partial remains of a 13- to 16-month-old calf, with a bovine skull placed in the head and other bones within the body. This cartonnage was likely used as a funeral object, either as a coffin lid or a sacred effigy in processions.
The pieces are located in the exhibition’s third section, “The Secrets of Saqqara”.
3. Kneeling Hatshepsut statue

Hatshepsut was one of the most successful pharaohs of the 18th dynasty (1550BC-1292BC) and one of the few female rulers who altered ancient Egyptian history.
What makes this statue so extraordinary is its deliberate blending of gender symbols: Hatshepsut is depicted with a bare masculine chest and wearing traditional male pharaonic regalia, complete with a shendyt kilt and the ceremonial false beard that symbolised divine kingship.
Her 21-year reign required careful political manoeuvring to legitimise her position as a female ruler in a male-dominated institution, making this interpretation of her role greatly significant and revolutionary.
The figure is on display in the first section, “The Land of the Pharaohs”.
4. Canopic jars used for organ storage

A set of four calcite canopic jars belonging to a noblewoman named Setyerboni offer a glimpse into the funerary practices of ancient Egypt.
Each was designed to hold and protect a specific internal organ removed during the mummification process, which the Egyptians believed was essential for the deceased’s rebirth in the afterlife, necessitating their preservation separate from the body.
Unlike earlier periods, where the organs were sometimes returned to the body cavity, the practice of using complete sets of individual jars became the standard for the elite.
An additional finding is an Imsety canopic jar belonging to Tjuyu, the mother-in-law of Amenhotep III, placed next to the collection.
They are held in the first section of the exhibition, “The Land of the Pharaohs”.
5. Stele with three scripts

A limestone stele with three scripts provides a window into a period of profound cultural and linguistic transition in ancient Egypt.
Dating from the Roman period, this stone slab features inscriptions in three distinct writing systems: traditional Egyptian hieroglyphs, the cursive Egyptian Demotic script and ancient Greek.
Beyond its linguistic utility, the stele stands as a powerful symbol of cultural synthesis and political pragmatism. Its very existence illustrates how the foreign Ptolemaic dynasty sought to legitimise its rule by engaging with and adopting Egyptian religious and bureaucratic traditions.
Like the Rosetta Stone, a well-known ancient Egyptian stele, this is another example of the continuity of Egyptian civilisation even under a new, external power.
The stone is placed in the fourth section, “Ancient Egypt and the World”.
“Ancient Egypt Unveiled: Treasures from Egyptian Museums”, Hong Kong Palace Museum, West Kowloon Cultural District, Tsim Sha Tsui, November 20, 2025, to August 31, 2026. Detailed programme and ticketing information can be found on the Hong Kong Palace Museum website.
